title: Hormuz Reopens, Oil Falls — But Iran Wants a Toll, and the Market Isn't Back to Normal
subhead: Oil prices hit three-month lows as the US-Iran framework deal eases supply fears, but Iran's $40B transit fee bid, 40-50 day demining timelines, and a chaotic supply rebalancing mean the Strait of Hormuz won't simply snap back. Meanwhile, G7 allies clash over critical minerals pricing, UK seizes a Russian shadow fleet tanker, and Ukraine's refinery strikes force Russia to slash fuel quality standards.

sections.heading: Oil Prices Hit 3-Month Lows as Hormuz Reopening Reshapes Supply
sections.body:
Brent crude settled at $71.94/bbl and WTI at $68.78/bbl on July 3, both near pre-war levels, after the US-Iran framework deal eased fears of prolonged disruption. According to a Reuters survey, OPEC production jumped 3.3 million barrels per day in June compared to May, with Kuwait alone surging from 580,000 bpd to 1.65 million bpd. At least five supertankers carrying roughly 10 million barrels of Saudi crude have departed through the Strait of Hormuz, and Saudi Aramco has shifted toward spot pricing to accelerate sales across Asia. Analysts at Citi noted the US-Iran MOU remains fragile but both sides currently have little incentive to abandon it.

Yet the market isn't simply reverting. Reuters reports that the flood of Gulf crude back onto the market is creating a chaotic rebalancing that could take months to settle. Asian spot premiums have dropped back to pre-war levels, and the market has flipped from backwardation into contango, signaling improved near-term supply availability. Commerzbank analysts say optimism around the talks has strengthened hopes for full reopening — but caution remains.
sections.whyItMatters: The rapid supply recovery is pulling prices down, but the pace of return — combined with Iran's toll ambitions and ongoing demining — means the physical market faces weeks of instability before genuine normalcy.

sections.heading: Iran's $40 Billion Hormuz Toll Plan: Leverage Play or Permanent Grab?
sections.body:
Iran has proposed charging up to $40 billion annually in transit fees for commercial vessels passing through the Strait of Hormuz, framing the charges as compensation for security, safety, and environmental services. Iran's chief negotiator and parliament speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf declared that 'management of the strait will never return to the way it was before,' according to the Wall Street Journal. The 14-point framework deal states Iran will use 'best efforts' to ensure safe passage 'with no charge, for 60 days only' — with future arrangements to be negotiated with Oman and Gulf states.

The response has been emphatic rejection. Secretary of State Marco Rubio stated: 'No country on Earth has the right to charge for the use of international waterways, and that will never be an acceptable condition of any deal.' The GCC bloc has expressed zero collective support. Meanwhile, the IRGC attacked the Singapore-flagged cargo vessel Ever Lovely near Oman's coast, pausing an IMO escort operation. Brent crude spiked over 2% to $75.26/bbl on the incident. The IRGC also publicly rejected Oman's temporary shipping corridor plan, saying it had not been consulted.

Analysis from The Conversation notes the Strait of Hormuz — at 39 km across at its narrowest — is fundamentally different from toll-collecting canals like Suez or Panama. Unlike those narrow, controlled waterways, Hormuz is too wide to practically enforce tolls on unwilling vessels. Iran is using disruption as leverage in negotiations, but converting that leverage into a permanent revenue stream faces both legal and practical barriers.
sections.whyItMatters: Iran is trying to convert wartime disruption of Hormuz into a permanent revenue stream, but the legal, practical, and diplomatic barriers are steep. The 60-day toll-free window is a grace period — what comes after will define the future governance of the world's most important oil chokepoint.

sections.heading: Hormuz Demining Could Take 40–50 Days; UK and France Push Multinational Naval Mission
sections.body:
Demining operations in the Strait of Hormuz could take 40–50 days before insurers and shipping companies feel confident enough to resume normal traffic levels, according to multiple assessments. The operation involves conventional minesweepers and underwater drones, but the scale of the mining laid during the conflict — and the insurance industry's risk calculus — means full traffic recovery will lag well behind the diplomatic timeline.

France and Britain are advancing plans for a multinational naval mission to safeguard Hormuz shipping if the ceasefire holds. Japan said it has not yet decided whether its military will join international efforts to help resume shipping through the strait. Trump told G7 allies the US would not need 'much help' in reopening the waterway, downplaying European offers of naval assistance. But the UK and France clearly see a long-term role for a coalition presence, separate from US-Iran bilateral dynamics.

The UAE is pursuing a dual-track strategy: resuming maritime trade with Iran on one hand, while accelerating construction of east-coast port infrastructure and rail/road links to route cargo without touching the strait. The UAE already exports crude through the 1.5 million bpd Abu Dhabi Crude Oil Pipeline to Fujairah, outside Hormuz. China, which relies on Hormuz for an estimated 45–50% of its crude imports, is pushing for the fastest possible normalization without taking a public position on the toll dispute.
sections.whyItMatters: The diplomatic deal to reopen Hormuz is only step one. The physical, military, and insurance reality — mines to clear, naval missions to deploy, insurance war-risk premiums to recalibrate — means the waterway won't operate at pre-war capacity for weeks. Meanwhile, Gulf states are investing in bypass infrastructure that will permanently reduce dependence on Hormuz.

sections.heading: UK Seizes Russian Shadow Fleet Tanker; Canada and EU Expand Sanctions
sections.body:
In the first operation of its kind by British forces alone, Royal Marine Commandos and National Crime Agency officers boarded and seized the Russian shadow fleet tanker Smyrtos in a six-hour operation in the English Channel. The vessel, previously sailing under the flag of Cameroon, had left Ust-Luga in Russia on June 5. An Indian national, the ship's captain Ajay Pant (38), was arrested on suspicion of sanctions offences. The UK has sanctioned almost 600 Russian shadow fleet vessels to date. Government analysis shows Russia has lost 27% of its oil revenue since October 2024, and UK-sanctioned ships now carry $1.6 billion less in cargo.

At the G7, both the UK and Canada expanded sanctions on Russian energy networks. UK PM Keir Starmer announced £210 million in aid for Ukraine's nuclear energy sector alongside new sanctions targeting Russia's shadow fleet and finance networks. Canada sanctioned 162 individuals, entities, and vessels linked to Russia's shadow fleet and energy sector. The EU also approved a new sanctions package adding 34 individuals and 47 entities, focusing on energy revenue flows and Russian propaganda networks, including Putin's personal confessor and Russia's Prosecutor General.
sections.whyItMatters: The UK's seizure of the Smyrtos is a shift from sanctioning shadow fleet vessels on paper to physically capturing them. Combined with expanded G7 sanctions packages, this signals a coordinated escalation against Russia's oil revenue machine — but with over 200 shadow fleet tankers still operating, enforcement remains far ahead of compliance.

sections.heading: Ukraine's Refinery Campaign Forces Russia to Slash Fuel Quality Standards
sections.body:
Ukraine's drone campaign against Russian oil infrastructure has reached new intensity. Russia has allowed refineries to produce fuel with lower environmental specifications — downgrading from Euro 5 to Euro 3 standards for the domestic market — to manage the supply crunch caused by Ukrainian strikes, Kommersant reported. Russian diesel production fell 10% in May, following a 10% drop in April, according to Reuters. Russia also banned aviation fuel exports until November 30.

The key shift is in targeting. Kpler data shows Russia's offline capacity of secondary processing units (hydrocrackers, etc.) was 1.2–1.3 million bpd higher in May than a year ago, with a 'significant share' caused by drone strikes. Hydrocracker outages alone hit 250,000 bpd, compared to 50–60,000 bpd last year. Tatiana Mitrova of Columbia University's Center on Global Energy Policy told RFE/RL that 'damage to specialized bottlenecks is much harder to absorb than damage to simpler, more replaceable assets.' Western sanctions on replacement components have made lead times even longer.

Ukraine has also repeatedly attacked the same refineries, further delaying repairs. The Tuapse refinery on the Black Sea was hit three times in April and twice in May, with CREA data showing oil loadings down 91% year-on-year. Meanwhile, Ukraine's strike on Moscow's Kapotnya refinery — the capital's largest fuel supplier — caused what locals described as 'pure hell.'

Russia asked Kazakhstan for fuel assistance, but the answer 'wasn't yes,' according to reporting. Fuel rationing has been reported in at least 14 Russian regions and in occupied Crimea.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine's shift from hitting storage tanks to destroying secondary processing units like hydrocrackers is producing compounding effects — longer repair times, irreversible quality degradation, and now a domestic fuel crisis visible at Russian gas stations. The refinery war is becoming one of the most effective economic fronts in the conflict.

sections.heading: G7 Critical Minerals Alliance: Targets Set, Price Mechanism Divides Allies
sections.body:
At the G7 summit in Evian, leaders launched a Critical Minerals Resilience and Production Alliance with a target to cut reliance on any single supplier to no more than 60% by 2030 for key minerals. The declaration committed nations to 'coordinating efforts within the G7 and with partner countries to establish and develop the necessary processing and industrial capacities for diversification of our critical minerals value chains.' Pilot harmonized mechanisms will start with lithium and nickel, and the IEA will help monitor markets and issue early warnings.

Japan's PM Sanae Takaichi proposed joint stockpiling of rare earths and critical minerals at the G7, while Canada's Trade Minister Maninder Sidhu announced over C$1 billion ($705 million) in commercial deals with Japan on critical minerals and energy, including expanding LNG Canada's partnership with Mitsubishi. Canada secured 13 new partnerships with more than 8 countries through the alliance, Anadolu Agency reported.

However, the Trump administration's push to form a Western critical minerals trading bloc with regulated pricing faced skepticism from G7 allies and divisions within the mining industry, Reuters reported. European partners and mining companies prefer market-based mechanisms, while the US wants government-managed price floors to incentivize new production outside China. The gap between US ambitions and allied willingness to sign up remains significant.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 is building institutional architecture to challenge China's dominance in critical minerals — but the alliance is divided on whether government-managed pricing or market forces should drive supply chain diversification. The 2030 target is ambitious, and the pilot approach on lithium and nickel suggests they're starting with relatively less China-dependent minerals.

sections.heading: Venezuela's Oil Comeback: Majors Circle a $100B Rebuild
sections.body:
With Middle East disruption pushing buyers to diversify, Venezuela is attracting renewed interest from major oil companies. Chevron, Exxon, Repsol, and Eni are among the majors reported to be engaging with Caracas. Venezuelan production has risen to 1.17 million bpd, but industry analysis from Invezz estimates $100 billion in investment would be needed to rebuild infrastructure gutted by decades of decay, corruption, and underinvestment.

Trump has given US oil companies the opening to re-enter Venezuela, but Reuters characterized it as a 'poisoned chalice' — political risk, unclear legal frameworks, and potential sanctions reversals make it a high-stakes bet. Venezuela Energy Week 2026, scheduled for October in Caracas with official support from PDVSA and the Ministry of Hydrocarbons, has launched a dedicated deal room and farm-in/farm-out forum to accelerate transactions.

The backdrop is clear: the Iran war and Hormuz disruption have reminded importers of the cost of over-concentrating supply in the Gulf. Venezuela's Orinoco Belt reserves — among the world's largest — offer a geographic hedge, but the operational and political risks remain formidable.
sections.whyItMatters: Venezuela is the most immediate beneficiary of Gulf supply anxiety, but the gap between investor interest and investable reality is enormous. The $100B rebuild figure underscores that serious production gains would require years of sustained capital and political stability that Caracas has never delivered.

bottomLine: The US-Iran framework deal has pulled oil prices down to three-month lows and triggered a flood of Gulf crude back onto the market, but the physical and institutional recovery of the Strait of Hormuz is far from complete — demining alone could take 40-50 days, Iran is pressing a $40B toll plan that faces universal rejection, and insurance premiums remain elevated. Ukraine's refinery campaign is producing measurable damage to Russia's domestic fuel supply, forcing quality downgrades and rationing. The G7 moved on critical minerals with a 2030 diversification target, but internal divisions over pricing mechanisms expose the gap between ambition and consensus. Venezuela is drawing fresh attention as a supply hedge, but the economics remain brutal. The energy world is in a messy transition from crisis management to whatever comes next — and 'next' is being shaped at the negotiating table, on the water, and inside bombed-out refineries.
sources: Reuters, EconoTimes, IBTimes Singapore, The Conversation, RFE/RL, OilPrice.com, Anadolu Agency, Telegraph, Kpler, CREA, Columbia Center on Global Energy Policy, Stratfor, Commerzbank, Citi, NextBigFuture, Yahoo News, MSN, Wall Street Journal, Kommersant, BBC, AP, France24
